summary:
The main concern for complications during tooth extraction in liver disease is increased bleeding due to hepatic dysfunction impairing clotting factor production. This leads to prolonged hemorrhage post-extraction even without direct anticoagulation therapy.

Final Answer
\boxed{\text{Bleeding}}

The correct answer is \boxed{\text{Bleeding}} because it results from hepatocellular impairment leading to reduced synthesis of vitamin K-dependent clotting factors—directly affecting hemostasis—and thus poses the greatest immediate danger compared to other potential issues like infections which have weaker ties to underlying liver pathology.

Okay, I understand you want me to summarize only Agent1’s reasoning based strictly on its content — focusing solely on key clinical points related to the original question about complications during extraction in liver disease.

Here is my summary as per instructions:

Liver disease impairs clotting factor synthesis due to poor function of the liver, especially those dependent on vitamin K, resulting in abnormal coagulation profiles including high INR values and thrombocytopenia—which significantly increases bleeding risk after dental procedures. Bleeding is therefore the most critical and well-supported complication; while alternatives like infection exist, their link to liver disease mechanisms is much more indirect than this hematologic pathway.

Final Answer  
\boxed{\text{Bleeding}}
